title: Iconicity, Convention, and Legibility
subtitle: How motivated visual form becomes disciplined writing
summary: Native anchor paper for the Writing and Representation in Sutton SignWriting series on visual motivation, convention, and legibility in SignWriting.
synopsis: This entry argues that iconicity is not the opposite of writing. It explains how visually motivated forms become writing when they are stabilized through convention, contrast, teachability, repeatability, correction, and reader practice. It distinguishes iconic language writing from free illustration, pictograms, emoji, and merely intuitive visual communication, treating SignWriting as a disciplined writing system whose iconic resources are organized by learned conventions.

slides.title: The Common Mistake
slides.summary: Iconicity is often mistaken for evidence that a system is not writing, but visual motivation and writing are not opposites.

image_prompts.concrete: Writing theory slide contrasting free illustration with conventional iconic written symbols in SignWriting
image_prompts.abstract: A visual sign passing through convention into writing

slides.speech: The common mistake is to treat iconicity as the opposite of writing. This paper argues the opposite: visually motivated form can become writing when it is stabilized through convention, contrast, correction, and reader practice.
slides.notes: Open by removing the false opposition.

slides.title: Not Mere Pictography
slides.summary: SignWriting uses iconic resources, but it is not free illustration, pictograms, emoji, or uncontrolled visual communication.

image_prompts.concrete: Comparison slide showing pictogram, emoji, free drawing, and SignWriting symbol composition as distinct visual systems
image_prompts.abstract: Visual resemblance refined into stable written contrast

slides.speech: The point is not that SignWriting lacks visual motivation. It plainly uses visual motivation. The point is that the visual forms are selected, conventionalized, placed, corrected, and reused as writing.

slides.title: What Convention Does
slides.summary: Convention turns motivated visual form into repeatable, teachable, contrastive written difference.

image_prompts.concrete: Academic slide showing motivated symbol forms becoming teachable categories, contrast sets, correction marks, and reader expectations
image_prompts.abstract: Visual motivation passing through a grid of convention

slides.speech: Convention does the stabilizing work. It turns visual motivation into readable difference by creating symbol choices, contrast sets, placement habits, teaching practices, and correction norms.
slides.notes: This is the center of the paper.

slides.title: Bridges
slides.summary: The paper connects plane-based writing and authored space to handwriting, machine encoding, and legibility research.

image_prompts.concrete: Bridge slide linking plane-based writing, authored space, iconicity, handwriting, machine encoding, and reader practice
image_prompts.abstract: Motivated form connecting theory, practice, and encoding

slides.speech: This paper is a bridge. It connects plane-based writing and authored space to the practical questions of legibility, handwriting, and machine encoding. Visual motivation matters because it is disciplined into written form.
slides.notes: Close by placing the paper in the series sequence.

slides.title: What Convention Does Not Solve
slides.summary: Convention makes iconic form writable, but it does not replace reader practice, evidence, handwriting study, or encoding work.

image_prompts.concrete: Boundary slide showing iconic form becoming conventional writing, with separate boxes for reader practice, evidence, handwriting, and encoding still requiring study
image_prompts.abstract: Visual motivation stabilized by convention while open research paths remain visible

bullets: Convention does not make reading automatic
Handwriting and encoding remain separate work
slides.speech: Convention solves one problem, not every problem. It explains how visually motivated form can become readable written contrast. It does not make reading automatic, replace evidence, settle handwriting practice, or solve machine encoding. Those remain related but separate questions.
slides.notes: This slide keeps the paper from overclaiming. It also makes clean bridges to handwriting, evidence, and Formal SignWriting.
